column7: message
Why not? Alice killed somebody while Bob merely injured someone. Doesn't Alice deserve a harsher sentence?

Others have covered many of the other points, but I couldn't see this:

Consider for a moment that I hit you or some other cyclist with my car, by accident.

Is it fair that I'm charged with manslaughter because you weren't wearing a helmet, where otherwise the crime and punishment would be less severe?

While yes, it would still be my fault for hitting you, should it also be my fault that you chose not to wear a helmet?

Having these laws in place just makes things a little easier for everyone involved. Sometimes laws really are there to protect you from yourself.

column7:
But we are not them, we are living in our own time, in 2017, and WE are deciding that the people of 2017 do not want to continue putting slavers on literal pedestrals.

When was the last time someone commissioned a statue of a Confederate general?

Also, if that is then the case then why are we not removing the Jefferson Monument in DC? Why are we not blasting Washington's face from Mt Rushmore? These men were slavers and fought for their right to own their slaves during the revolutionary war as part of their war for independence (or did you miss the part of history where England banned slavery in the early 1700's and Great Britain right around the time of the revolution?)

Removing statues is not about rewriting history. We are not pretending to remove statues retroactively a hundred years ago, *we are removing them today*, for our own sake. 

It's one thing to say, that American slavers were respectable in their own time.*  But we are not them, we are living in our own time, in 2017, and WE are deciding that the people of 2017 do not want to continue putting slavers on literal pedestrals. If the peope of the past considered slavery an ambigous issue, that's their business, but WE don't. WE have a right to say that for us, slavery is a black and white issue, a monstrous crime, and that WE consider the ones who fought for it, to be monsters, and that WE don't want to venerate them in OUR public spaces. 

It's one thing to understand, that there once was a time when most people didn't share that morality, but we are not obliged to keep agreeing with them. 

Understanding that different eras have different moralities, swings both ways. We have to understand that the past's values were different from ours, but also that our values are different from the past, and not beholden to it.  

---
 *Although that's not really correct either. Chattel slavery always did have a fierce abolitionist opposition on a moral ground. John Brown didn't die for abstract "economic reasons", but for a virtous cause that he believed in. Preachers didn't say from the pulpit, that “The tree of Abolition is evil, and only evil—root and branch, flower and leaf, and fruit; that it springs from, and is nourished by an utter rejection of the Scriptures”, because of some detached, morally neutral economic reasons. Plenty of people in the 19th century have believed that slavery was a black and white issue, it's just that we are now more uniformly clear on which side was the black one and which side was the white.

@soft-union So basically you're saying we should erase all the bad stuff from history and pretend it never happened? Sounds like you wanna live in a fantasy world where everything is rainbows and unicorns. What about the good things those people did? Should we forget about that too? And what's next, renaming cities and states? Get real.
"@random-liquor Let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the issues rather than resorting to personal attacks or dismissive remarks. Constructive dialogue is key."

column7:
> How will you keep corruption out of government?

I'm not sure why Libertarianism would have any significant impact on the *desire* for those in government to be corrupt.  But if the government is smaller and has less access to money, there is less available for corruption.

> How will you address long term global affects or territorial affects? Killing all the deer of a region...global warming....

If this is something important to individuals, then individuals will form together and come to an agreement to regulate themselves.  We all agree to only kill one deer each year.  If someone violates that, or doesn't want to participate, we essentially "boycott" them which more or less ensures their participation.  If there are enough people who choose to not participate that boycotting is ineffective, then it is clear that society as a whole doesn't actually care much about that issue.

Same thing with global warming.  If it actually mattered to people, you wouldn't need government forced regulations.  That's just a reflection of the people in government forcing their own personal beliefs to the population they govern.

> How do you deal with situations in which mass action or inaction is making it worse for all people? Sanitation is the biggest historical example imo. People used to throw stuff in their backyard and in the street.

People who don't want garbage throughout their neighborhood would gather together and come to an agreement about sanitation - same "boycott" principle as above.  And those people who want to throw garbage in their backyard are able to live in areas with similar-minded people and put their garbage wherever they want.

I really appreciate your response.

>I'm not sure why Libertarianism would have any significant impact on the desire for those in government to be corrupt. But if the government is smaller and has less access to money, there is less available for corruption.

Not everyone will agree with their government so it doesn't matter if libertarians want corruption in their government people will try to put corruption in their government, that's a guarantee. If the government is smaller? Well wouldn't a military be necessary no matter what? If there is not much government structure and power then the chances for a militaristic take over is very high. You have a general that wants power then bam you have a massive war that affects innocents. If you are saying no military or police then how in the world do you propose to stop wealthy individuals from just gunning down people and having the money to get away with since there will be people that do not have the same ideology and can be bought out. Boycotting individuals isn't an option when they own all the food in the area or have built a wall around the area stopping you from leaving or doing a number of more subtle methods that have happened over the years.


>If this is something important to individuals,...a whole doesn't actually care much about that issue.

The thing is, if a company kills the deer of an area and the indigenous individuals have food issues and have starvation issues then those people have been affected negatively by that company. Now people may not like that it happened but they may not know. What if the company purchases land around the area and prevents any trespassing. That is within the scope of possibility within libertarian oriented government. This company has no liability and has effectively murdered children and no one will know because of laws in effect to protect the privacy of the owners of the company. 



>Same thing with global warming. If it actually mattered to people, you wouldn't need government forced regulations. That's just a reflection of the people in government forcing their own personal beliefs to the population they govern.


So are you saying that government should only act on things people actually care about? Global warming has already and will continue to cause issues for people around the globe even those who do not participate in the pollution. Yet you are saying that the government should not take any action. Would you say the same thing if someone were to break another persons legs. The person with broken legs is upset but every other person does not care because the person breaking their legs pays everyone else to not care. No body cares enough about that persons sovereignty to take action. People will only care about what they have the means to care about. If they are barely getting by since food is restricted then that's what they will care about, they won't care that some dude is being killed. Even if that is against a libertarian tenant. 




>That's just a reflection of the people in government forcing their own personal beliefs to the population they govern.

The overwhelming proof for the extent and cause for climate change is not up for debate my conviction of that will not be changed. You questioning that will only convince me that you are expressing naivete through willful ignorance. That is not a point I came here to discuss, and why it is not listed as an opinion. 


>People who don't want garbage throughout their neighborhood would gather together and come to an agreement about sanitation - same "boycott" principle as above. And those people who want to throw garbage in their backyard are able to live in areas with similar-minded people and put their garbage wherever they want.

The whole boycott idea is an unproven assumption I would love to see your evidence that change has happened regularly while relying entirely on grassroots movements without a default system in place to implement this change.

You are also not addressing the idea that the masses don't know it is bad for them in the proposed scenario and since they have known nothing else have no other objections. The people in a position to learn ALL have potential monetary gain in NOT spreading the information. There is no incentive to help or teach.  Assuming that someone who would be in a position to know would work and sacrifice their free time to help others on a regular basis seems entirely unreasonable.

@threadbare-rotation Wow, you really think small government leads to military takeovers and people getting gunned down? Sounds like a comic book plot! And the deer thing? Companies killing off deer and starving people? That’s some wild storytelling right there. As for global warming, if it’s such a big deal, why aren’t people just banding together to fix it? Seems like a lot of hand-wringing over nothing if folks aren’t stepping up. Boycotting isn’t just an assumption, it’s how things get done in the real world. Grassroots movements are the backbone of change. If people don’t care enough to act, maybe it’s not as urgent as you think. Superhero costumes optional, but activism definitely required! 🦸‍♂️

column7:
The argument put forward seems like "No True Scotsman" - the people you are condemning claim to be Christian, attend Christian churches, follow Christian pastors (who espouse the same views they hold), etc.  They _are_ Christians, though perhaps not the specific sect of Christian that you are.

Now, that aside, the problem with scripture as I see it is that we can cherry pick quite a bit to justify your possitions.  Sure, Jesus told us to take care of the sick, the widows and the children, but he also said "He who will not work shall not eat" in Thessalonians 3:10.  That can be used to justify a stance against welfare despite the message of love elsewhere in the Bible; Jesus himself said you shouldn't expect to get help from others if you are not willing to work yourself.  Similarly, the letters of Paul (which are canonical in Christianity) have a great deal to say about many of of the things that you are calling out as non compassionate - while you can love the person and their soul, Christians are _commanded_ to try and stop behavior that is immoral.

In summation, they may not fit your personal definition of Christian, but that doesn't make them unChristian.

I don't believe it is a logical fallacy, because my belief is that Republican values are not Christian values.  

Going against a Christian value is unchristian, regardless of how you label the individual. 

To avoid the problem of responses that cherry pick scripture, I defined Christianity in terms of its greatest, or core, principle, as defined by Jesus.  Those who are angry or hateful toward homosexuals are objectively unchristian in that behavior.  If you disagree on this point, I don't think your arguments will be persuasive.  

Lastly, Christians are not commanded to create laws to punish anyone who disagrees with them.  I don't believe those who approach the problem this way are really concerned with helping the individual.  We don't lock people up for years because we want to help them.
column19: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Takes work calls at any time of the day']

column7: @lime-lark I understand where you're coming from, but I truly believe that actions speak louder than words. If someone claims to follow Christian values but then shows hate or anger towards others, especially based on who they love, it's hard to reconcile that with the teachings of love and acceptance from Jesus. It's not just about what's written in the Bible, but how those teachings are lived out in everyday life.

column7:
>You're still describing the actions of people taken in favor of the ideology, not the pitfalls of the ideology itself.

In that case, I would argue that you've subtly misinterpreted the target of the "popular critiques" of the social justice movement that you refer to in your title. The critiques are *precisely* against the "actions of people taken in favor of the ideology", not the ideology itself. The goal of feminism, in its purest, most benevolent form, might be "to achieve equality for women". Although that's an extremely vague statement, virtually no one opposes that ideal.

I don't lay my critique against feminism the *ideology*, I lay it against feminism the *practice*. You can say that these two entities shouldn't be lumped together, and this is certainly a good point, but then we hit an unfortunate semantic ambiguity. When *I* use the word "feminism", I'm talking about the practice, but when *you* use the word, you're talking about the ideology. So when you read critiques of feminism, what you are really reading is a critique of the practice, but you treat it as though they are critiques of the ideology and then (rightly) conclude that the critiques don't hold up!

Once you accept that the intended target of the critique is the *practice* of social justice, which invariably is connected to its *practitioners*, it's not so easy to dismiss the arguments set forth by the other commenters in this thread.
